title: The Big Bang Of AI Just Happened: Cosmos 3
summary: NVIDIA launched Cosmos 3, a world model that trains on video, audio, text, and action data to enable robots to understand and predict physical environments. Cosmos 3 is part of NVIDIA's full physical AI stack alongside Vera and the Isaac GR00T humanoid robot.
writeup: NVIDIA announced three interlocking pieces of a physical AI stack: Cosmos 3, a world foundation model trained on 20 trillion multimodal tokens that can simulate physical environments and cut robot training cycles from months to days; Vera, a CPU built explicitly for agentic AI workloads, claimed to finish diverse agent tasks up to 1.8x faster than x86; and the Isaac Groot open humanoid reference robot, pairing a 75-degrees-of-freedom chassis with Jetson AGX Thor compute and tactile five-finger hands. The through-line is NVIDIA's bid to become the operating layer for physical AI, the same platform lock it achieved for GPU compute, while the field's urgency sharpens: Foundation Future Industries has already field-tested humanoid robots in Ukraine for logistics operations near hazardous areas.
key_points: Cosmos 3 is trained on 20 trillion tokens of multimodal data including real and synthetic video, ambient audio, and action trajectories from both humans and robots, allowing it to predict future physical states rather than merely describe current ones.

NVIDIA claims Cosmos 3 can compress physical AI training and evaluation cycles from months down to days, addressing the fundamental bottleneck that robot training cannot tolerate millions of real-world failures due to hardware damage and safety risks.

Vera is positioned as the first CPU purpose-built for agentic AI, with Anthropic, OpenAI, xAI, ByteDance, CoreWeave, and Oracle Cloud Infrastructure named as planned adopters; Jensen Huang described it as addressing a potential $200 billion market.

The Isaac Groot reference humanoid stands nearly 6 feet tall, weighs 150 lb, delivers 75 degrees of freedom across body and dual Sharpa tactile five-finger hands, and runs 270 TOPS of AI compute via the Jetson AGX Thor T5000 Blackwell GPU onboard.

Hands are singled out as the hardest unsolved problem in humanoid robotics: manipulation, not locomotion, determines whether a humanoid can work in spaces designed for human bodies, making the 22-degree-of-freedom tactile hands a load-bearing design choice.

Foundation Future Industries tested its Phantom Mark1 humanoid in Ukraine for logistics tasks in hazardous areas, and the company has discussed future combat roles, representing a deployment reality far ahead of warehouse or manufacturing timelines.
